title: High Protein Black Bean Quesadillas
metaDescription: Crispy black bean quesadillas with 24g protein per serving — mashed black beans, melted cheese, and a crispy tortilla shell. Ready in 15 minutes.
author: Recipe Team
keywords: black bean quesadilla, vegetarian protein snack, high protein lunch
ingredients: 2 cans black beans, drained and rinsed
Salsa and Greek yogurt, for serving

steps.name: Mash the beans
steps.text: In a bowl, roughly mash the black beans with a fork, leaving some texture. Stir in cumin, garlic powder, chili powder, lime juice, and a pinch of salt.

steps.name: Assemble the quesadillas
steps.text: Lay out 4 tortillas. Spread the mashed bean mixture evenly over each, then sprinkle with shredded cheese. Top each with a second tortilla.

steps.name: Cook until crispy
steps.text: Heat a thin layer of olive oil in a large skillet over medium heat. Cook each quesadilla for 2-3 minutes per side, pressing down gently, until golden and crispy and the cheese is fully melted.

steps.name: Slice and serve
steps.text: Let cool for 1 minute, then slice into wedges. Serve with salsa and a dollop of Greek yogurt on the side.

tips.question: Can I make these ahead and reheat them?
tips.answer: Yes — cooked quesadillas keep in the fridge for up to 3 days. Reheat in a dry skillet for 2-3 minutes per side to restore the crispy exterior instead of using the microwave, which makes them soggy.

tips.question: How do I boost the protein even more?
tips.answer: Add 1/2 cup of cooked, shredded chicken or a handful of extra shredded cheese to push each serving past 30g of protein.

tips.question: Can I use corn tortillas instead of flour?
tips.answer: Yes, though corn tortillas are smaller and more prone to cracking — warm them briefly in a dry pan first to make them more pliable before assembling.

tips.question: Why mash instead of leaving the beans whole?
tips.answer: Mashing helps the filling hold together when sliced, instead of whole beans spilling out of the quesadilla with every bite.

storyIntro: Quesadillas are usually a cheese delivery vehicle with barely any protein backing it up. Mashing a full can of black beans into the filling changes that math completely — the beans hold the filling together and add close to half the protein in the whole recipe.
It's a 15-minute meal that works as a fast lunch, an appetizer cut into small wedges, or a kid-friendly dinner that happens to have real nutrition behind it.
storyNote: Press down gently while the quesadilla cooks — that light pressure is what gets the cheese fully melted before the tortilla over-browns.

images.heroAlt: Crispy black bean quesadilla wedges with melted cheese and salsa on the side
